title: The Hiddenness of God in Suffering
description: Why does God sometimes seem absent in our darkest moments? A theological meditation on Deus absconditus — the hidden God. We explore the honest prayers of the psalmists, Luther's theology of the cross, and the promise buried within divine silence.
outline: Luther and the Theology of the Cross
Practical Postures in the Dark Night

sections.title: The Complaint of the Psalmists
sections.scriptureText: Wake up! Why do you sleep, Lord? Arise! Don't reject us forever. Why do you hide your face, and forget our affliction and our oppression? For our soul is bowed down to the dust. Our body clings to the earth. Rise up to help us. Redeem us for your loving kindness' sake.
sections.reflection:
The psalms of complaint are among the most uncomfortable texts in the Bible for Christians who have been taught that prayer should always be reverent, measured, and optimistic. Yet here is the inspired Word of God commanding God to wake up — as if he were asleep on the job. The psalmist does not soften the accusation. He levels it directly: you have hidden your face. You have forgotten us.

This is not the language of weak faith. It is the language of faith pressed to its limits and still holding on. The great Walter Brueggemann has argued that the psalms of lament represent what he calls 'countertestimony' — a refusal to domesticate God into a deity who always behaves as we expect. The psalmist knows God well enough to be furious with him.

The church has often been nervous about this voice. We prefer the psalms of praise, the psalms of confident trust. But Bonhoeffer, writing from prison, observed that we cannot hear the psalms of praise rightly unless we have also prayed the psalms of lament. They belong together. The cry of dereliction on the cross — 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — is itself a quotation of Psalm 22. Jesus prayed this prayer, which means that no depth of felt abandonment is outside the range of legitimate prayer.

The pastoral implication is crucial: we must give suffering people permission to pray with the full vocabulary of the psalms. To tell a grieving person that they should just trust God and feel better is to muzzle the very prayers God inspired. The complaint psalms exist precisely because God is not afraid of our anger. He is large enough to receive it, and faithful enough not to be undone by it. In fact, the very act of complaining to God — rather than about God — is itself an act of faith. You do not bother complaining to someone you believe cannot hear you.
sections.prayer: Lord, we confess that we have sometimes felt as the psalmist felt — abandoned, forgotten, hidden from your face. We do not understand your silences. But we bring our complaints to you, not away from you, because we believe you are there to receive them. Teach us to pray honestly, to cry loudly, and to hold fast even when we cannot feel your presence. For your name's sake, arise and help us.

sections.title: Luther and the Theology of the Cross
sections.scriptureText: Most certainly you are a God who hides himself, God of Israel, the Savior.
sections.reflection:
Martin Luther's Heidelberg Disputation of 1518 introduced one of the most important distinctions in Reformation theology: the difference between a theology of glory and a theology of the cross. The theologian of glory looks for God where God seems most impressive — in power, in beauty, in success, in answered prayer, in miracles. The theologian of the cross has learned to find God precisely where God seems most absent: in weakness, in suffering, in the cross.

Isaiah 45:15 is the scriptural anchor for this tradition. 'Truly you are a God who hides himself.' This is not a lament about God's absence — it is a theological confession. The God of Israel hides himself. He is Deus absconditus, the hidden God, and this hiddenness is not a malfunction of his providence but a feature of it. He does not reveal himself where human wisdom expects to find him.

Luther pushed this further in his commentary on the Psalms and in his pastoral letters. He distinguished between the hidden God — the raw, unapproachable majesty of God in himself — and the revealed God, who comes to us clothed in the flesh of Jesus Christ. The danger, Luther argued, is when we try to climb up to the hidden God by speculation, mysticism, or moral achievement. That path leads to despair or pride. The safety is in coming down, stooping to the manger, clinging to the cross.

For Luther, the theology of the cross was not a morbid preoccupation with suffering. It was a radical reorientation of where we look for God. When your life feels empty and God feels distant, Luther would say: do not look inward for spiritual warmth, do not look upward for celestial visions — look at the cross. God has hidden himself in the most scandalous place imaginable, so that no one can boast of having found him by their own cleverness or virtue. The hidden God comes to the humble, to those who have run out of other options.
sections.prayer: God who hides yourself in weakness and in wood, forgive us for seeking you only in the places of our own choosing — in the bright and comfortable, in the answered and the certain. Train our eyes to find you where you have promised to be found: in the Word, in the sacraments, in the neighbor who suffers, and above all in the cross of your Son. Drive us from the theology of glory to the theology of the cross.

sections.title: What Hiddenness Is Not
sections.scriptureText: "Behold, I go forward, but he isn't there; backward, but I can't perceive him; on the left hand, when he works, but I can't see him. He turns to the right hand, but I can't see him. But he knows the way that I take. When he has tested me, I will come out like gold.
sections.reflection:
Job's famous speech in chapter 23 is one of the most theologically careful descriptions of divine hiddenness in Scripture. He is not saying that God does not exist. He is not saying that God is indifferent. He is saying that God cannot be found — at least not by Job's searching. East, west, north, south: wherever Job looks, God is not there.

And then, in one of the great theological pivots of the entire book, Job says: 'But he knows the way that I take.' Job cannot find God. But God has not lost Job. This is the crucial distinction that must be maintained in any careful treatment of divine hiddenness. God's hiddenness to us is not the same as our hiddenness to God. He is not lost. We are not invisible to him. The asymmetry is absolute.

This matters for how we counsel those in suffering. When someone says 'I cannot feel God,' we should not immediately diagnose this as sin, or lack of faith, or spiritual failure. Job was a man of extraordinary faith — God himself said so — and yet Job could not find God. The hiddenness was real. The suffering was real. And God was still watching, still knowing, still present even in his apparent absence.

What hiddenness is not: it is not abandonment. It is not punishment in every case (the book of Job demolishes the simple retributive theology of his friends). It is not evidence that prayer has failed or that faith is broken. And it is not permanent. Job's speech ends with a confession of hope: 'When he has tested me, I will come out like gold.' The refiner's fire is not the same as the executioner's fire. One purifies; the other destroys. Job, in the depths of his hiddenness, somehow held onto the distinction — and that holding-on is itself a form of faith.
sections.prayer: Heavenly Father, remind us in our searching that though we cannot always find you, you have never lost us. You know the way we take. You see what we cannot see. Help us to rest in the asymmetry of your knowing — that our ignorance of your ways does not mean your ignorance of ours. We trust that the testing is not the end, and that you are refining, not destroying.

sections.title: The Promise Within the Silence
sections.scriptureText: For he has not despised nor scorned the affliction of the afflicted. He hasn't hidden his face from him, but when he cried to him, he heard.
sections.reflection:
Psalm 22 begins with the most desolate cry in the Psalter: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' It is the cry that Jesus made his own from the cross — and this is theologically staggering. The incarnate Son of God, in his dying moment, prayed the prayer of the abandoned. Whatever this means theologically (and centuries of careful theology have been spent here), it means at minimum that the experience of forsakenness is real enough that even the Son of God prayed through it.

But Psalm 22 does not end in forsakenness. It turns — not cheaply, not quickly, but it turns. And the turn arrives at verse 24, which is one of the most precious promises in the entire Psalter: 'He has not despised nor scorned the affliction of the afflicted. He has not hidden his face from him, but when he cried to him, he heard.'

The God who seemed to hide his face was listening all along. The silence was not abandonment. The hiddenness was not rejection. The very cry that seemed to go unanswered was, in fact, received — and the one who cried was heard.

This is the promise buried within the silence. It cannot be accessed by spiritual optimism, by positive thinking, or by the decision to feel better about God. It is accessed only by what Luther would call naked trust — the raw decision to keep praying even when prayer seems to bounce off the ceiling, to keep believing even when belief feels like a bare grip on nothing.

For those walking through a dark night of the soul, this verse is not a quick fix. It is a long promise. It is the assurance that God's ear is never closed even when his face is hidden. The posture of the sufferer, then, is to keep crying — not because crying feels productive, but because the one to whom we cry has promised to hear.
sections.prayer: Lord, in our silences, we cling to this: that you have not despised our affliction, that you have not turned away. Even when we cannot feel your presence, let us trust your promise. You heard the cry of your Son from the cross. You will hear ours. Give us grace to keep praying through the darkness until the dawn breaks — as it must, because you are faithful.

sections.title: Practical Postures in the Dark Night
sections.scriptureText: It is because of Yahweh's loving kindnesses that we are not consumed, because his compassion doesn't fail. They are new every morning. Great is your faithfulness.
sections.reflection:
Lamentations is one of the most devastating books in the Bible. Jerusalem has fallen. The temple has been destroyed. The people are in exile. The poet of chapter 3 has been through the worst imaginable — 'He has led me and made me walk in darkness, and not in light' (3:2). And yet, in the middle of this catharsis of grief, the poet pauses and confesses: the mercies of the Lord are new every morning.

This is not spiritual bypassing. The poet does not pretend that things are fine. He has just spent twenty-one verses describing his suffering in unflinching detail. The 'great is your faithfulness' comes after the lament, not instead of it. This is the grammar of mature Christian faith: honest grief followed by stubborn trust.

What are the practical postures for living through a season of divine hiddenness? Several suggest themselves from the wisdom of Scripture and the Christian tradition. First, maintain the disciplines even when they feel empty. Pray even when prayer feels dead. Read Scripture even when it seems opaque. Attend worship even when God feels absent. These are not acts of works-righteousness but acts of positioning — staying in the place where God has promised to meet his people, trusting that the feelings will follow in his time.

Second, find companions for the journey. Elijah under the juniper tree was not sent more theology — he was given food, rest, and eventually a companion in Elisha. God meets bodily needs in bodily ways during seasons of spiritual drought.

Third, resist the interpretive rush. Not every season of hiddenness has a legible meaning that can be discerned in the moment. Sometimes the meaning comes only in retrospect, if at all. The posture is not understanding but endurance — and endurance, Paul tells us, produces character, and character produces hope.

Great is his faithfulness. This is not a feeling. It is a declaration. And declarations can be made in the dark.
sections.prayer: God of every morning, whose mercies are new when ours are exhausted, teach us to declare your faithfulness even before we feel it. Keep us in the disciplines when the disciplines feel dry. Give us companions for the dark valleys. And when we cannot understand your ways, give us the grace of endurance — until we see, on that final morning, what you were doing all along.

title: What Calvin Taught Us About Justification
description: Calvin's treatment of justification in Institutes Book III remains among the clearest and most pastoral expositions ever written. We walk through his key arguments, from the definition of justification to the relationship between faith and works, guided always by Scripture.
outline: What Justification Is and Is Not
Faith as the Instrument of Justification

sections.title: What Justification Is and Is Not
sections.scriptureText: But now apart from the law, a righteousness of God has been revealed, being testified by the law and the prophets; even the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ to all and on all those who believe. For there is no distinction, for all have sinned, and fall short of the glory of God; being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus; whom God set forth to be an atoning sacrifice, through faith in his blood, for a demonstration of his righteousness through the passing over of prior sins, in God's forbearance; to demonstrate his righteousness at this present time; that he might himself be just, and the justifier of him who has faith in Jesus.
sections.reflection:
Calvin opens his treatment of justification in the Institutes (III.11) with characteristic precision: justification is 'the acceptance with which God receives us into his favor as if we were righteous; and we say that it consists in the forgiveness of sins and the imputation of Christ's righteousness.' The definition is dense, but every word carries weight.

First, note what justification is not. It is not sanctification — the moral transformation of the believer. Calvin was acutely aware that confusing these two realities leads either to moralism (trying to be justified by progressive holiness) or antinomianism (believing that justification removes the obligation to grow in grace). He insists the two must be distinguished even though they are never separated. The same Christ who justifies also sanctifies, but these are two different benefits of union with him.

Second, note what justification is: it is forensic. It is a legal declaration. God the judge pronounces the sinner righteous — not because the sinner has become righteous, but because the righteousness of Christ has been credited to the sinner's account. Paul's language in Romans 3 is judicial throughout: 'being justified,' 'the justifier,' 'redemption,' 'atoning sacrifice.' These are courtroom and temple words.

The radical logic of Romans 3:26 is worth pausing over: God is both just and the justifier of the ungodly. How can this be? How can a perfectly just God declare guilty sinners righteous without compromising his justice? Paul's answer is the cross: Christ bore the penalty that justice demanded, so that justification can be offered freely without justice being violated. This is the hinge on which the whole gospel swings, and Calvin saw it with extraordinary clarity.
sections.prayer: Lord God, just and justifier, we thank you that in Christ you have found a way to be true to your own righteousness while embracing us in ours. We have nothing to offer you but the record of our failure. Yet you have credited to us the perfect obedience of your Son. Receive our gratitude, and let this doctrine not remain in our heads alone but descend, as Calvin prayed, into our hearts.

sections.title: The Double Grace (Duplex Gratia)
sections.scriptureText: But of him, you are in Christ Jesus, who was made to us wisdom from God, and righteousness and sanctification, and redemption.
sections.reflection:
One of Calvin's most celebrated contributions to Reformation theology is his doctrine of the duplex gratia — the double grace. When a sinner is united to Christ by faith, they receive two distinct but inseparable gifts: justification (the forgiveness of sins and the imputation of Christ's righteousness) and sanctification (the renewal of the inner person by the Holy Spirit). Paul captures both in one compressed verse: Christ is made to us 'righteousness and sanctification.'

Calvin's genius was to hold these together without collapsing them into each other. The Roman Catholic tradition of his day tended to merge justification into sanctification — righteousness was infused into the believer progressively, so that justification was partly a matter of moral transformation. Some antinomian Protestants, swinging in the opposite direction, separated them so radically that sanctification became optional. Calvin's duplex gratia cuts between both errors.

The key is union with Christ. We do not receive justification from Christ and sanctification separately as two discrete gifts. We receive Christ himself, and in him we receive both. You cannot have Christ for forgiveness only, as if you could extract one benefit from him while refusing another. To be in Christ is to receive the whole Christ — and the whole Christ both justifies and sanctifies.

This has profound pastoral implications. The person struggling with persistent sin should not be told: 'Try harder; your justification depends on it.' Justification is complete and certain in Christ. But they should also not be told: 'Relax; you're justified, so your behavior doesn't matter.' The same Christ who bore your sins is also at work by his Spirit remaking you from the inside out. Both truths are required for a full-orbed gospel.
sections.prayer: Christ Jesus, our wisdom, our righteousness, our sanctification, our redemption — we thank you that you give yourself whole to us, not in pieces. Forgive us for clinging to your justification while resisting your sanctifying work, or for confusing the two until we have lost the assurance of the first. Grant us grace to live in the fullness of union with you, receiving both gifts with gratitude.

sections.title: Faith as the Instrument of Justification
sections.scriptureText: We, being Jews by nature, and not Gentile sinners, yet knowing that a man is not justified by the works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ, even we believed in Christ Jesus, that we might be justified by faith in Christ, and not by the works of the law, because no flesh will be justified by the works of the law.
sections.reflection:
The Reformers insisted that justification is by faith alone — sola fide — and this phrase was one of the great battle lines of the sixteenth century. But Calvin was careful to qualify what this means, and his qualification is theologically essential: faith itself does not justify us. It is Christ who justifies us. Faith is the instrument by which we receive Christ and all his benefits.

This distinction matters enormously. If faith justifies us, then faith is itself a kind of work — a spiritual achievement, a contribution we make to our own salvation. And then the question becomes: is my faith strong enough? Am I believing correctly enough? Is the quality of my trust sufficient? This road leads to endless introspection and eventual despair.

Calvin's answer was to redirect attention away from the quality of our faith and toward the object of our faith. A weak hand that grasps a strong rope saves a drowning man just as surely as a strong hand. What saves is the rope — that is, Christ. Faith is the empty hand that receives the gift; it contributes nothing to the gift itself.

Paul in Galatians is devastatingly clear: 'no flesh will be justified by the works of the law.' This includes not only the ceremonial law but the moral law — not only ritual performance but ethical achievement. There is no category of human effort, however sincere or sustained, that can produce justification before God. The only path is through faith in Jesus Christ — faith that looks away from the self and rests entirely on what Christ has done.

Calvin saw this not as a doctrine that produces passivity but as the only doctrine that produces genuine freedom. When you know that your standing before God does not depend on your performance, you are free to act from love rather than from fear.
sections.prayer: Lord Jesus, we confess that we are prone to turn even our faith into a work — to examine it, measure it, doubt whether it is sufficient. Remind us again that you are the object, not our believing. The hand that reaches for you does not need to be strong. It needs only to reach. Give us grace to look away from ourselves and rest entirely on your finished work.

sections.title: Imputed Righteousness — Christ's, Not Ours
sections.scriptureText: Yes most certainly, and I count all things to be a loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus, my Lord, for whom I suffered the loss of all things, and count them nothing but refuse, that I may gain Christ and be found in him, not having a righteousness of my own, even that which is of the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness which is from God by faith.
sections.reflection:
Paul's autobiographical confession in Philippians 3 is one of the most dramatic renunciations in Scripture. He had credentials that would have commanded respect in any Jewish community: circumcised on the eighth day, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, a Pharisee of exemplary zeal. These were genuine achievements. They were real. And yet Paul calls them 'refuse' — the Greek word skubalon, which is as strong as it sounds — compared to the surpassing worth of knowing Christ.

Why? Because Paul has discovered the distinction between his own righteousness and the righteousness that comes from God through faith. These are not different quantities of the same thing; they are different kinds of thing altogether. Paul's own righteousness, however impressive, is self-generated. It originates in Paul's effort, Paul's discipline, Paul's devotion. The righteousness that comes from God through faith originates in Christ — and it is Christ's perfect obedience, credited to Paul's account, that Paul now stands in.

Calvin developed this insight into the doctrine of the 'double imputation': our sins are imputed to Christ on the cross (so that he bears their penalty), and Christ's righteousness is imputed to us in justification (so that we receive its benefit). This is the great exchange — what Luther called the fröhliche Wechsel, the joyful exchange.

The pastoral weight of this doctrine cannot be overstated. The Christian stands before God not in their own spiritual progress but in Christ's perfect record. On the worst day of your spiritual life, when you have failed repeatedly and feel furthest from God, your standing before him has not changed — because it was never based on your performance to begin with. It was always based on his.
sections.prayer: Father, what relief this truth brings — that we are found in him, not having a righteousness of our own. We come to you not with our record but with his. Not with our obedience but with the obedience of your beloved Son. Let this truth quiet the anxious heart, silence the voice of accusation, and fill us with the freedom of those who have nothing to prove.

sections.title: How Works Relate to Justification
sections.scriptureText: What good is it, my brothers, if a man says he has faith, but has no works? Can faith save him? And if a brother or sister is naked and in lack of daily food, and one of you tells them, "Go in peace, be warmed and filled;" and yet you didn't give them the things the body needs, what good is it? Even so faith, if it has no works, is dead in itself.
sections.reflection:
James 2 has long been the Reformed tradition's most challenging exegetical test. If justification is by faith alone, what do we do with James's declaration that 'faith without works is dead'? Luther famously struggled here, and at one point questioned whether James belonged in the canon at all. Calvin's approach was more carefully exegetical and ultimately more satisfying.

Calvin's key insight was contextual: James and Paul are answering different questions. Paul in Romans and Galatians is addressing the ground of justification — what makes a person right before God. His answer: faith in Christ, not works of the law. James is addressing the evidence of justification — how we can know that someone's faith is genuine. His answer: genuine faith produces works. These are not contradictory but complementary.

The faith that justifies, Calvin insisted, is never alone. It is always accompanied by its fruit. But the fruit does not contribute to the justification — it demonstrates it. Works are the necessary consequence of genuine faith, not its cause. To use an analogy Calvin himself employed: we are justified by faith alone, just as the sun alone gives light — but the sun that gives no heat is not a real sun. A faith that produces no works is not real faith.

This understanding releases Christians from both legalism and antinomianism. You are not working to earn or maintain your justification. But you are also not free to ignore the call to love your neighbor, pursue justice, and grow in holiness. You work because you are justified, not in order to be justified. The motivation shifts from fear and self-promotion to gratitude and love — and that shift, Calvin would say, makes all the difference in the character of the work produced.
sections.prayer: Lord, save us from the dead faith that says the right words but does nothing with the neighbor in need. And save us from the anxious works that try to earn what you have already freely given. Kindle in us a living faith — the kind that justifies before you and also moves through us toward the world in mercy. Let our works be the natural overflow of hearts that have understood grace.

title: Learning to Pray with the Psalms
description: Dietrich Bonhoeffer called the Psalter 'the prayer book of Jesus.' What happens when we let the Psalms teach us how to address God? This study walks through Brueggemann's three movements and the surprising power of praying texts we did not write.
outline: Why the Psalms Are Our Language School
Praying Imprecatory Psalms as a Christian

sections.title: Why the Psalms Are Our Language School
sections.scriptureText: Trust in him at all times, you people. Pour out your heart before him. God is a refuge for us.
sections.reflection:
When we learn a new language, we begin not by improvising but by imitation. We repeat phrases, practice constructions, inhabit grammars we did not invent until they become our own. The Psalms function this way in the spiritual life. They are God's gift to us of a language for prayer — a vocabulary large enough for every human emotion, and a grammar shaped by covenant faith.

Dietrich Bonhoeffer's short book Psalms: The Prayer Book of the Bible makes the startling claim that the Psalms are not primarily our prayers to God but God's word to us about how to pray. They are Christ's own prayer book — he prayed them as a faithful Jew, and he prays them still as our great high priest and intercessor. When we pray the Psalms, we are joining a prayer already in progress, a prayer offered by the Son to the Father in the power of the Spirit.

This reframes the question of whether we can 'mean' every psalm we pray. Sometimes a psalm expresses exactly what we feel. But sometimes it expresses what we do not feel yet — and that is equally valuable. To pray 'great is your faithfulness' when you do not feel it is not hypocrisy; it is discipleship. We are training our souls in the grammar of trust by submitting to a language larger than our present emotional state.

Psalm 62:8 gives the essential invitation: 'Pour out your heart before him.' This is remarkably uninhibited language. Not 'compose your thoughts and present them politely.' Not 'approach with appropriate spiritual decorum.' Pour out your heart — which means bring the whole mess, the anger and the grief and the confusion and the gratitude, and let it go before the one who is a refuge. The Psalms give us permission to pray as full human beings.
sections.prayer: Father, thank you for the Psalms — for this school of prayer that has taught your people across thirty centuries how to cry and praise and confess and trust. Teach us to inhabit this language, to let it reshape our prayers from the inside. When our own words fail, let us borrow the words of the Psalms, knowing that in doing so we are praying with your people and with your Son.

sections.title: Psalms of Orientation (Praise and Trust)
sections.scriptureText: Praise Yahweh, my soul! All that is within me, praise his holy name! Praise Yahweh, my soul, and don't forget all his benefits, who forgives all your sins, who heals all your diseases, who redeems your life from destruction, who crowns you with loving kindness and tender mercies, who satisfies your desire with good things, so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's.
sections.reflection:
Walter Brueggemann's influential typology of the Psalms identifies three movements: orientation, disorientation, and reorientation. Psalms of orientation are those that affirm the goodness and order of creation, the reliability of God's covenant, and the basic trustworthiness of the world as God has made it. Psalm 103 is among the most magnificent of these.

Note the structure of the opening verses: David is commanding his own soul to praise. This is not the spontaneous overflow of feeling but a deliberate act of will. 'Forget not all his benefits' — the implication being that the soul is always in danger of forgetting. Memory is the engine of praise. When we rehearse what God has done — forgiven, healed, redeemed, crowned, satisfied, renewed — we are rebuilding the foundations of trust.

Brueggemann is careful to note that orientation psalms are not naive. They do not pretend that suffering does not exist. They affirm order and goodness precisely because that order and goodness can be doubted. To say 'great is your faithfulness' in the morning is to stake a claim against the chaos that night can bring.

For Christian practice, the psalms of orientation give us the habit of praise as a discipline. This is not toxic positivity. It is the daily practice of deliberately setting our attention on what is true about God before we encounter what is difficult about the day. The ancient practice of morning prayer — beginning the day with praise and thanksgiving before petition — was shaped by exactly this instinct. We orient ourselves toward God before the day disorients us.

Psalm 103 also reminds us that praise is not vague. It is specific. David does not say 'God is good in general.' He says: he forgives this, he heals this, he redeems from this, he crowns with this. Specific praise produces specific faith.
sections.prayer: Lord, we bless your holy name — for forgiveness that covers all we have done, for mercy that meets us in our illness, for redemption from the pit we have dug for ourselves. Train us in the discipline of morning praise. Let us not forget your benefits. Renew our youth like the eagle's, and carry us through the disorientations of this day by the memory of your steadfast love.

sections.title: Psalms of Disorientation (Lament)
sections.scriptureText: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Why are you so far from helping me, and from the words of my groaning? My God, I cry in the daytime, but you don't answer; in the night season, and am not silent.
sections.reflection:
The psalms of disorientation are those that arise from the collapse of the ordered world the orientation psalms assumed. Grief, illness, betrayal, persecution, national catastrophe — these are the contexts from which lament emerges. And the Psalter is full of them. By some counts, more than a third of all psalms are laments. This is not a minor key in the Psalter; it is a dominant mode.

Psalm 22 is the paradigmatic lament, and its opening verse is the most quoted line from the Psalms in the New Testament — because Jesus prayed it from the cross. This creates a remarkable interpretive situation: the lament that feels most like abandonment is the prayer that the Son of God himself prayed in his darkest hour. Whatever Psalm 22 means, it means that lament is christologically sanctioned. It is a form of prayer that Jesus himself modeled.

The structure of Psalm 22 repays careful attention. The psalmist cries out (vv. 1-2), then recalls God's past faithfulness (vv. 3-5), then returns to the present crisis (vv. 6-18), then turns to petition (vv. 19-21), and finally arrives at praise (vv. 22-31). This is not a logical argument but a spiritual process — the soul working its way from abandonment to trust, not by suppressing the pain but by bringing it fully before God.

For many Christians, the psalms of lament are the most liberating texts in the Bible, because they give permission to be honest with God. The church has often cultivated a culture of spiritual cheerfulness that leaves no room for those who are in the depths. The psalms of lament are a pastoral corrective. They say: your darkness has a language, and God gave it to you. Use it.
sections.prayer: God of the forsaken cry, we thank you that even the worst of our prayers — the prayers of abandonment and anger and grief — are not outside the range of what you have sanctioned. You gave us Psalm 22. You prayed it yourself. Receive our laments as you received your Son's, and be present in the depths of our darkness, working even there toward the praise that is coming.

sections.title: Psalms of Reorientation (Thanksgiving)
sections.scriptureText: Out of the depths I have cried to you, Yahweh. Lord, hear my voice. Let your ears be attentive to the voice of my petitions. If you, Yah, kept a record of sins, Lord, who could stand? But there is forgiveness with you, therefore you are feared.
sections.reflection:
Psalms of reorientation are those that emerge on the other side of suffering — not back to naive orientation, but forward into a deeper, harder-won trust. The world has not gone back to what it was. But God has been found faithful in the midst of the disruption, and the psalmist testifies to that faithfulness from the far side of the valley.

Psalm 130, the De Profundis, is one of the most beloved of all penitential psalms. It begins in the depths — that word captures everything: the deep place of sin, of suffering, of felt separation from God. And from those depths, the psalmist cries. Not from the heights of spiritual achievement, not from the plateau of moral improvement, but from the bottom.

The theological movement in verses 3-4 is breathtaking: 'If you kept a record of sins, Lord, who could stand?' The answer is obvious: no one. No one at all. And then the pivot: 'But there is forgiveness with you.' This is not earned; it is given. The same God who keeps perfect moral accounts also holds in his nature the capacity — the delight — to forgive. And the stunning pastoral note is that this is the basis of fear: 'therefore you are feared.' The fear of God is not primarily terror before wrath; it is awe before mercy so unexpected and so complete that it commands reverent wonder.

Reorientation psalms do not promise a return to normalcy. They testify to the discovery of a God who is present in the abnormal — in the depths, in the waiting, in the long night of soul. They are the testimonies of those who have come through, not around, the darkness, and found that God was in it.
sections.prayer: Out of the depths we cry to you, Lord. Hear our voices — strained from crying, weak from waiting. We know we cannot stand before you on our own record. But there is forgiveness with you, and on that forgiveness we rest everything. Teach us to wait with the night-watchmen for the morning, knowing that the morning comes. Your steadfast love does not yield to our deserving.

sections.title: Praying Imprecatory Psalms as a Christian
sections.scriptureText: Let their table before them become a snare. May it become a retribution and a trap. Let their eyes be darkened, so that they can't see. Make their backs continually to shake. Pour out your indignation on them. Let the fierceness of your anger overtake them. Let their habitation be desolate. Let no one dwell in their tents.
sections.reflection:
The imprecatory psalms — those that call down divine judgment on enemies — are the most theologically difficult texts in the Psalter for Christian use. Can we really pray these? Should we? They seem to contradict the commands of Jesus to love enemies and pray for those who persecute us.

Several approaches have been offered. The first is simple avoidance: skip these psalms, they are sub-Christian. But this fails to take seriously the inspiration of Scripture and the canon's integrity. The second is allegory: the enemies are spiritual forces, not human beings. This has ancient pedigree but feels like a way of dodging the historical specificity of the text.

The most theologically robust approach involves several moves. First, recognize that imprecation is the cry of those who have been seriously wronged — often catastrophically wronged — and who refuse to take vengeance into their own hands. By praying the imprecation rather than acting on it, the psalmist is actually handing the justice to God. 'Vengeance is mine; I will repay, says the Lord' (Romans 12:19). The imprecatory psalm is the liturgical form of that surrender.

Second, recognize that Christ has prayed these psalms and absorbed their curse. The New Testament cites Psalm 69 in reference to Jesus (John 15:25, Romans 15:3). In some sense, the judgment the psalmist prays for has fallen — on Christ, who bore the sins of those who persecuted him and his people.

Third, recognize that Christians can pray these psalms on behalf of the persecuted church globally. When brothers and sisters are suffering unjust violence, it is appropriate to bring that injustice before God and ask for his justice. This is not personal vindictiveness; it is covenant advocacy.

The imprecatory psalms remind us that God is a God of justice, and that justice matters. They keep us from a sentimentality that pretends evil does not deserve judgment. Prayed in Christ, they become prayers for the vindication of the righteous and the defeat of all that destroys human flourishing — a prayer for the kingdom to come.
sections.prayer: Lord of justice, we do not fully understand these psalms, but we will not hide from them. We bring before you the injustice done to your people across the world — the persecuted, the wrongly imprisoned, the victims of violence. We do not take vengeance into our own hands. We put it in yours. Come quickly, Lord Jesus, and make all things right.

title: The Strange Logic of Grace
description: Grace defies all economic logic. It cannot be earned, repaid, or deserved. This study explores why the gospel is so hard to believe and so easy to forget, through the lens of the parable of the prodigal son and Paul's letters.
outline: Why We Revert to Merit Thinking
Living Under Grace Without Abusing It

sections.title: The Prodigal — Reading It Correctly
sections.scriptureText: He arose, and came to his father. But while he was still far off, his father saw him, and was moved with compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. The son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in your sight. I am no more worthy to be called your son.' But the father said to his servants, 'Bring out the best robe, and put it on him. Put a ring on his hand, and sandals on his feet. Bring the fattened calf, kill it, and let us eat, and celebrate; for this, my son, was dead, and is alive again. He was lost, and is found.' They began to celebrate.
sections.reflection:
The parable of the prodigal son is so familiar that we have largely lost our capacity to be shocked by it — which means we have largely lost the parable. Kenneth Bailey's landmark work on the Middle Eastern cultural context of the story helps restore the scandal. In the ancient Near East, a father would never run. Running was beneath the dignity of a patriarch. The sight of an older man hiking up his robes and sprinting down the road would have been mortifying to witness.

But that is exactly what this father does. He sees his son while the son is still far off — which implies the father has been watching, waiting, scanning the horizon every day. When he sees him, before the son can deliver his rehearsed speech, before any accounting can be made, the father runs. He falls on the son's neck. He covers the son's shame with his own body before the village can see and mock.

The robe, the ring, the sandals, the feast — each one is a statement of restoration, not just forgiveness. The robe is the best one, given before any work is done. The ring is a signet of authority — the son is not welcomed back as a servant but as a son. The sandals are what distinguished a free man from a slave. The feast is a public celebration of what the village would have considered humiliating: this disgraced young man is being treated as if he had done nothing wrong.

This is the strange logic of grace. It does not wait for the sinner to prove himself. It runs. It covers. It restores before any restitution is made. Jesus told this parable to Pharisees and scribes who were scandalized that he welcomed sinners and ate with them. He is not defending his behavior — he is revealing the character of the Father.
sections.prayer: Father, we confess that we find this story almost too good to believe. We keep expecting the father to require something — a season of probation, a period of demonstrated change, some token of earned trust. But you run before we can earn anything. Receive our gratitude for a grace we cannot contain, and let its logic begin to reshape how we extend that grace to others.

sections.title: Why We Revert to Merit Thinking
sections.scriptureText: Foolish Galatians, who has bewitched you? Before your eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified! I just want to learn this from you: did you receive the Spirit by the works of the law, or by hearing of faith? Are you so foolish? Having begun in the Spirit, are you now completed in the flesh?
sections.reflection:
Paul's frustration with the Galatians is palpable and instructive. These were people who had received the gospel, believed it, experienced the Spirit's work — and then drifted back into a merit-based framework under the influence of teachers who said that faith in Christ needed to be supplemented by Torah observance. Paul calls this madness: having begun in the Spirit, are you trying to be made complete by the flesh?

The Galatian error is not unique to first-century Judaism. It is the default setting of the human heart. Every culture develops its own system of merit — ways of earning standing, accumulating credit, and justifying oneself. In religious contexts, these systems can look very spiritual: bible reading, prayer disciplines, service hours, doctrinal correctness. These are good things. But the moment they become the basis of our standing before God — or our sense of spiritual superiority over others — they have become the Galatian error in new clothing.

Why do we revert to merit thinking? Several reasons suggest themselves. First, grace is simply too strange to sustain. Our entire economic and social world runs on exchange, on reciprocity, on earned rewards. Grace violates all of these intuitions, and our minds keep trying to normalize it back into something manageable. Second, grace is threatening to the ego. If my standing before God is entirely a gift, then there is nothing to be proud of. The ego resists this strenuously. Third, merit thinking gives us control. If I can earn blessing, I am not dependent on a sovereign God who gives as he will. I am in the driver's seat.

The antidote Paul prescribes is not moral effort but theological memory: 'Before your eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified.' Look at the cross again. You were not saved because you were deserving. You were not kept because you have been faithful. You received the Spirit not as a reward but as a gift. Remember this — and remember it again tomorrow.
sections.prayer: Lord, expose in us the merit thinking we dress up as devotion. Show us where we have made our spiritual practices into a currency we spend before you, expecting a return. Bring us back to the beginning: before our eyes, Christ crucified. We began in the Spirit; keep us there. Do not let the flesh complete what the Spirit started.

sections.title: Grace Is Not Permissiveness
sections.scriptureText: What shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound? May it never be! We who died to sin, how could we live in it any longer? Or don't you know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him through baptism into death, that just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we also might walk in newness of life.
sections.reflection:
The objection Paul anticipates in Romans 6 is always the objection that arises when grace is preached clearly: if grace covers all sin, then why not sin freely? If God's forgiveness is unlimited, what stops us from exploiting it? This is not a hypothetical concern — it is a live temptation, and Paul addresses it with his characteristic rhetorical force: 'May it never be!'

His answer is not a moral lecture about the importance of behaving well. It is a theological argument about the nature of what happened to us in Christ. If you have been united with Christ in his death, you have died to sin. Not merely died to sin's penalty — died to sin's power. The old self that was enslaved to sin has been crucified with him. You are not the same person who sinned freely before. You have been transferred from one dominion to another.

This is Paul's point throughout Romans 6: sin is no longer your master. You are not under its jurisdiction. The question 'shall we continue in sin?' is not merely morally wrong; it is theologically incoherent. It is like asking whether a freed slave should return voluntarily to their former master's fields. Technically possible, but a complete denial of who they now are.

Grace is not permission to sin — but this is not because grace is stingy with forgiveness. It is because grace has done something more radical than forgive: it has remade us. The person who truly understands that they are buried with Christ and raised with him no longer finds sin's invitation attractive in the same way. Sin promises what it cannot deliver to someone who has tasted resurrection life. Grace is not the removal of law's demands but the fulfillment of what law always pointed toward: a people who want to walk in newness of life.
sections.prayer: Lord, keep us from using your grace as a license. Remind us that we have died with Christ, and that the one who died is no longer at home in the old patterns of sin. When temptation offers us what grace supposedly allows, remind us of who we are: buried and raised, transferred and renewed. Let the newness of life be not just a doctrine we hold but a reality we inhabit.

sections.title: The Elder Brother's Error
sections.scriptureText: But he was angry, and would not go in. Therefore his father came out, and begged him. But he answered his father, 'Behold, these many years I have served you, and I never disobeyed a commandment of yours, but you never gave me a goat, that I might celebrate with my friends. But when this, your son, came, who has devoured your living with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him.'
sections.reflection:
The elder brother is the character in the parable who is most often overlooked — and he is the one who most resembles the original audience. Jesus told this parable to Pharisees and teachers of the law who were grumbling about his table fellowship with sinners. The elder brother is them.

He has done everything right. He has stayed. He has obeyed. He has served faithfully. And what has he received? Apparently not even a goat for a party with friends. And now his wastrel brother comes home, and there is a feast. The elder brother's indignation is humanly understandable. By the logic of merit, he is absolutely right: this is not fair.

But Tim Keller's insight in The Prodigal God is essential here: the elder brother's obedience was itself a form of self-seeking. He was not serving the father out of love — he was serving the father as a transaction, expecting commensurate reward. His obedience was a tool for self-justification. He was more like his younger brother than he realized: both were trying to get the father's goods for themselves. The younger brother tried through rebellion; the elder tried through religion. Both missed the point.

The elder brother's error is the error of all religious moralism: it keeps score, it compares, it resents grace extended to the undeserving because secretly it believes it deserves grace itself. The father's response is both tender and corrective: 'Son, you are always with me, and all that I have is yours.' The elder brother has been living in the father's house as if he were a servant waiting for wages, when in fact he already had everything. He just could not enjoy it, because his eyes were on the ledger instead of on the father's face.
sections.prayer: Father, forgive us for the elder brother that lives in us — the part that keeps score, resents the grace shown to those we judge undeserving, and serves you with one eye on the reward. You have given us everything in your Son. Teach us to live in your house as beloved children, not as servants waiting for wages. And give us the grace to celebrate when you run toward the lost.

sections.title: Living Under Grace Without Abusing It
sections.scriptureText: For by grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, that no one would boast. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared before hand, that we should walk in them.
sections.reflection:
Ephesians 2:8-10 is the canonical summary of the relationship between grace and works. Verses 8-9 are the fortress of the Protestant gospel: saved by grace through faith, not of works, not of yourselves. This is the sola gratia and sola fide in concentrated form. Verse 10 is the completion that prevents the doctrine from collapsing into license: we are created for good works, which God prepared beforehand for us to walk in.

The logic moves in one direction: grace produces works, not the reverse. Works do not produce grace. They cannot, because grace is by definition that which is not earned. But grace that produces no works is a grace that has not actually taken root. Paul's phrase 'his workmanship' — the Greek poiema, from which we get 'poem' — suggests that we are God's artistic creation, his crafted masterpiece, made for a purpose.

Living under grace without abusing it means holding both halves of this verse simultaneously. Verse 8 prevents the moralism that tries to earn God's favor through religious performance. Verse 10 prevents the antinomianism that treats grace as permission for passivity or sin. The two halves together describe the life of a person who knows they are completely accepted by God (v. 8) and therefore completely available to God (v. 10).

The practical form of this life is one shaped by gratitude rather than fear. You do not do good works to secure your standing — that standing is secure in Christ. You do good works because you have been remade by grace for exactly this purpose, and because those works flow naturally from a heart that has been liberated from the slavery of self. Grace does not abolish obligation; it transforms its motivation from compulsion to delight.
sections.prayer: God of grace, we receive the gift and refuse the debt. We are saved and we know it — by grace, through faith, not from ourselves. And now, Lord, show us the good works you prepared for us before we knew you. Make us your poem, written in the world by lives that reflect your character. Let our works be the evidence of grace received, not the currency by which we try to purchase it.

title: Owen's Mortification — A Study Guide
description: Walking through John Owen's 'The Mortification of Sin' (1656) — still the most rigorous treatment of how Christians fight indwelling sin. This study translates Owen's seventeenth-century theology into practical guidance for contemporary believers.
outline: The Main Argument: Sin Must Be Killed

sections.title: The Main Argument: Sin Must Be Killed
sections.scriptureText: For if you live after the flesh, you must die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.
sections.reflection:
John Owen opens his treatise on mortification with one of the most arresting sentences in Puritan literature: 'Be killing sin or it will be killing you.' This is not rhetorical flourish. It is a careful reading of Romans 8:13, which presents the Christian life as a sustained and mortal conflict. There is no neutrality in this conflict. The believer who is not actively putting sin to death is being slowly killed by it.

Owen's main argument is that mortification — the active, habitual weakening of the power of sin in the believer's life — is not optional for Christians. It is not an advanced spiritual discipline for the especially devout. It is the ordinary Christian life. Paul frames it as a conditional: 'if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.' The life that flows from the Spirit is characterized by this ongoing killing work.

The word 'mortification' comes from the Latin mors, death, and facere, to make. To mortify sin is to put it to death — not to wound it, not to negotiate with it, not to manage it at an acceptable level, but to kill it. Owen is relentless about this. Any arrangement with sin — any toleration, any scheduled indulgence, any comfortable accommodation — is a defeat. Sin must be killed.

But note the instrument: 'by the Spirit.' Owen is equally insistent that mortification is the Spirit's work, not our own. The believer is the agent, but the Spirit is the power. This means that mortification is neither pure passivity (wait for God to take sin away) nor pure human effort (grit your teeth and try harder). It is cooperative — the Spirit providing the power, the believer wielding it in specific acts of faith, resistance, and discipline.
sections.prayer: Holy Spirit, we cannot kill our sin alone. Our flesh is too strong and our wills too weak. But we take up the weapons you have provided — prayer, Scripture, accountability, the body of Christ — and we ask you to make them effective. Teach us to be killing sin rather than accommodating it. And do not let us grow complacent in the conflict.

sections.title: Who Can Mortify Sin
sections.scriptureText: But I say, walk by the Spirit, and you won't fulfill the lust of the flesh. For the flesh lusts against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh; and these are contrary to one another, that you may not do the things that you desire.
sections.reflection:
Owen's second major point is that only a believer can truly mortify sin — and this point is as important as his first. He is not describing a self-improvement program available to any motivated person. He is describing what happens in the life of someone who has been united to Christ by the Spirit and in whom the Spirit genuinely dwells.

Galatians 5 describes the interior conflict that every genuine believer experiences: the flesh and the Spirit are at war within the same person. This conflict — which Paul describes as an ongoing, unresolved tension — is actually evidence of life. The person who experiences no inner conflict between the pull of sin and the pull of the Spirit should be more alarmed, not less. The absence of conflict may indicate the absence of the Spirit.

Owen drew a sharp distinction between what he called 'common grace' and 'special grace.' A person under common grace — a morally serious, religiously motivated unbeliever — can restrain sin outwardly. They can exhibit impressive self-control. They can reform their behavior in dramatic ways. But they cannot mortify sin in Owen's sense, because mortification requires the indwelling Spirit, the life of Christ in the soul, the new nature that has been created by regeneration.

This means that calls to mortification directed at unbelievers are pastorally misguided. Telling someone who has not been born again that they need to work harder at killing their sin is to give them a task they cannot perform and a standard they cannot meet. The first word must always be the gospel: be reconciled to God, receive the Spirit, enter into union with Christ. Then and only then does the call to mortification make sense — and then, crucially, it becomes a call to use a power already given rather than a power yet to be obtained.
sections.prayer: Lord, thank you for the indwelling Spirit who makes the fight possible. Where we have tried to mortify sin in our own strength — through willpower and resolution and self-discipline alone — we have failed and will fail again. Lead us to depend not on our determination but on the Spirit's power. And give us honesty to recognize the conflict within us as evidence of your life, not our failure.

sections.title: What Mortification Is Not
sections.scriptureText: If you died with Christ from the elements of the world, why, as though living in the world, do you subject yourselves to ordinances, "Don't handle, nor taste, nor touch" (all of which perish with use), according to the precepts and doctrines of men? These things indeed appear to have wisdom in self-imposed worship, and humility, and severity to the body; but are of no value against the indulgence of the flesh.
sections.reflection:
Owen's treatise includes a crucial negative section: he specifies what mortification is not, because the most common attempts at it are precisely the things the Bible warns against. Paul's words in Colossians 2 are devastating: 'Do not handle, do not taste, do not touch' — these rules appear to have wisdom. They look like serious spirituality. But they are 'of no value against the indulgence of the flesh.'

Mortification is not willpower. The person who grits their teeth and resolves never to sin in a particular way again, relying solely on their own determination, will eventually fail — and the failure will be worse for the overconfidence that preceded it. Owen is scathing about those who think that a sufficiently strong will can put sin to death. The will is itself corrupted by sin and cannot be the instrument of its own healing.

Mortification is not bodily severity. Fasting, sleep deprivation, physical discomfort — these can be valid spiritual disciplines when ordered toward prayer and dependence on God. But Owen follows Paul in warning that asceticism can become a form of spiritual pride rather than genuine mortification. The monk who flagellates himself may be more entrenched in self-righteousness than before he began.

Mortification is not redirection. Some people deal with a besetting sin by replacing it with another absorbing activity — a new hobby, a new ambition, a new social cause. The original sin appears to go quiet. But Owen argues this is not mortification; it is suppression. The sin has been distracted, not killed. It will resurface when the distraction fails.

True mortification, Owen argues, goes to the root: it seeks to weaken the habit of sin by means of the gospel — by a fresh apprehension of Christ's love, the Spirit's power, and the real ugliness of what sin is doing to the soul.
sections.prayer: Lord, expose in us the counterfeit mortifications we use to manage our sin rather than kill it — the rules we follow to feel disciplined, the austerities we perform to feel spiritual, the replacements we find to feel free. Take us to the root. Apply the gospel to the place where sin lives. And do not let us settle for the appearance of victory when the war is still being fought inside.

sections.title: The Eight Duties of Mortification
sections.scriptureText: Put to death therefore your members which are on the earth: sexual immorality, uncleanness, depraved passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry.
sections.reflection:
Owen's positive program of mortification involves a series of duties — specific practices and habits by which the believer, in dependence on the Spirit, weakens the power of indwelling sin. While Owen lists these somewhat differently across his writings, they can be summarized under several heads.

First, consider the dangerous nature of your sin. Owen insists that we must look carefully at what sin is actually doing to us — its guilt before God, its damage to the soul, its tendency to harden the heart. We must not let ourselves be charmed by sin's promises while ignoring its consequences. Second, load the conscience with the guilt of sin — not to the point of paralysis or despair, but to the point of genuine sorrow that motivates flight from sin rather than accommodation to it.

Third, long for deliverance from the power of sin. This is more than wishing sin were less troublesome. It is the whole-hearted desire to be free, to be holy, to be more like Christ. Owen believed that many who claim to want freedom from sin actually want only relief from its consequences. The longing for holiness itself is a gift of the Spirit and a sign of genuine regeneration.

Fourth, consider whether the sin is rooted in your particular temperament and make special provisions accordingly. Some sins prey on particular weaknesses. Paul's command in Colossians 3 — 'put to death therefore your members' — suggests specificity. Not sin in general but these particular members.

Fifth — and for Owen perhaps most importantly — meditate on the cross. A fresh sight of Christ crucified, of what his death says about the seriousness of sin and the extent of God's love, is the most powerful mortifying force available. The person who is deeply affected by the cross will find the pleasures of sin shrinking in comparison to the beauty of the one who died for them.
sections.prayer: Spirit of holiness, apply Owen's wisdom to our specific sins. Show us what each sin is costing us, load our consciences with honest sorrow, and kindle in us a genuine longing for your holiness. Bring us again and again to the cross, where sin is shown for what it is and grace is shown for what it is. Let the sight of Christ crucified do what all our resolutions could not.

sections.title: Applying Owen Today
sections.scriptureText: Beloved, I beg you as foreigners and pilgrims, to abstain from fleshly lusts, which war against the soul.
sections.reflection:
Peter's appeal in 1 Peter 2:11 situates the call to mortification in its proper eschatological frame: we are 'foreigners and pilgrims.' We are not at home here. The world around us is not our final environment, and its values, its pleasures, and its priorities are not ultimately determinative for how we live. The fleshly lusts that war against the soul are not fighting from a position of strength — they are fighting against people who belong to another country.

How does Owen's seventeenth-century program translate to contemporary life? Several applications present themselves. The digital age has created categories of temptation Owen could not have imagined — the endless scroll, the algorithmic reinforcement of appetite, the constant availability of pornography, the addictive structures of social media's approval economy. These are new delivery mechanisms for old sins: lust, pride, envy, sloth, the craving for recognition. Owen's analysis applies perfectly; the technology has changed, not the sin.

The principle of exposure management — putting no confidence in the flesh, removing occasions for sin — applies directly to digital life. This might mean using internet filters, establishing rules about device use in bedrooms, deleting apps that function as occasions for sin, or building in accountability structures with trusted friends. Owen would recognize all of these as legitimate means of grace in the war against temptation.

Community remains essential. The Puritans practiced what we might call structured accountability — regular meeting with fellow believers to confess sin, pray together, and speak honestly about the state of the soul. The anonymity of contemporary life works against mortification. Isolation is sin's friend; community is its enemy.

Above all, Owen's central counsel applies in every age: stay close to Christ. A fresh encounter with the love of God in the gospel — through Scripture, prayer, the Lord's Supper, the preaching of the Word — is the most reliable mortifying force available. Be killing sin or it will be killing you. But never fight alone.
sections.prayer: Lord, we live as pilgrims in a world that offers us everything our flesh desires. Give us the pilgrim's long view — the knowledge that we are passing through, that this is not our home, and that the pleasures of sin are thin and temporary compared to the joy set before us. Make us diligent in the fight, wise in avoiding occasions for sin, and constant in bringing ourselves back to you when we fail.

title: The Hope of the Resurrection
description: The resurrection of Jesus Christ is not merely a past event but the foundation of Christian hope for the future. This study explores what the New Testament teaches about resurrection — Christ's and ours — and how that hope reshapes the way we live now.

sections.title: Death Is Not the End
sections.scriptureText: But now Christ has been raised from the dead. He became the first fruits of those who are asleep. For since death came by man, the resurrection of the dead also came by man. For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ all will be made alive.
sections.reflection:
Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15 is the most sustained and rigorous treatment of resurrection in the New Testament. He begins with the absolute centrality of the claim: if Christ has not been raised, your faith is worthless and you are still in your sins (v. 17). But then, having acknowledged the disastrous consequences of resurrection's falsity, he pivots with one of the great 'but now' moments in Scripture: 'But now Christ has been raised from the dead.'

The language of 'first fruits' is agricultural and liturgical at once. In Israel's worship calendar, the first fruits were the initial offering of the harvest — not the whole harvest, but its guarantee. To offer the first fruits was to declare that the rest of the harvest was coming. Christ's resurrection is the first fruits of the general resurrection: it is not an isolated event but the beginning of the end of death itself.

Paul's Adam-Christ typology here is crucial for understanding the scope of the resurrection hope. Death entered the world through Adam — not merely biological death, but the complex of alienation, corruption, and mortality that sin introduced into God's good creation. Christ's resurrection is the reversal of this. 'As in Adam all die, so also in Christ all will be made alive.' The 'all' in both clauses is not universalism but scope: death's domain is wide, and so is resurrection's promise for those in Christ.

What this means practically is that Christian hope is not hope for escape from the material world but hope for its renewal. The resurrection body is a body — not a ghost, not a pure spirit, not a disembodied soul floating in some ethereal heaven. The future that God has promised is material, embodied, physical. This world matters because this world will be redeemed.
sections.prayer: Lord of the resurrection, remind us that death is not the final word. Christ's empty tomb is the guarantee of our own. We grieve — we will grieve — but not as those who have no hope. For as in Adam we have tasted death, so in Christ we will taste life, fully and forever. Let this hope sustain us through every loss and every ending we face.

sections.title: The Resurrection Body
sections.scriptureText: So also is the resurrection of the dead. The body is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption. It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power. It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body. There is a natural body, and there is also a spiritual body.
sections.reflection:
Paul uses a series of contrasts in verses 42-44 to describe the difference between the present body and the resurrection body: corruption vs. incorruption, dishonor vs. glory, weakness vs. power, natural vs. spiritual. The contrasts are not between material and immaterial — the resurrection body is still a body — but between the present mode of embodied existence and its transformed, glorified form.

'Spiritual body' is a phrase that has caused much confusion. It does not mean a body made of spirit, as opposed to a body made of matter. Paul uses the same word 'spiritual' (pneumatikos) elsewhere to describe things that are controlled by or oriented toward the Spirit — 'spiritual gifts,' 'spiritual food,' 'spiritual songs.' A spiritual body is a body fully under the dominion and life-giving power of the Holy Spirit, no longer subject to the limitations and corruptions of the present age.

C.S. Lewis captures something of this in The Great Divorce, where the visitors from heaven are described as more solid, more real, more substantial than the ghosts of the earthly realm. The resurrection body will not be less than our present bodies — it will be more. More fully alive, more fully integrated, more fully capacitated for the worship and work and fellowship that God designed embodied creatures for.

For those who suffer in their bodies — chronic illness, disability, the slow erosion of age — this is not a promise to escape the body but a promise that the body will be redeemed. The very body that now causes suffering will be raised in power and glory. This is a breathtaking hope, not a spiritual platitude. It means that nothing done in or through the body in faith is wasted.
sections.prayer: Father, we live in bodies that break, that ache, that age, that ultimately die. But you have promised a resurrection — not an escape from bodies but the redemption of them. Thank you for this strange and magnificent hope. Help us to honor our bodies now as temples of the Spirit and as seeds of the glory to come. And help us comfort one another with these words.

sections.title: Living Backwards from the Resurrection
sections.scriptureText: If then you were raised together with Christ, seek the things that are above, where Christ is, seated on the right hand of God. Set your mind on the things that are above, not on the things that are on the earth. For you died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God. When Christ is revealed, who is our life, you will also be revealed with him in glory.
sections.reflection:
Paul's argument in Colossians 3 is characterized by a 'therefore' logic: because of what is true about you in Christ, live accordingly. The Christian life is not lived forward, earning status by accumulating virtue. It is lived backwards from a completed reality: 'you were raised together with Christ.' The resurrection is not just future — it has already been applied to believers in their union with Christ.

The command to 'set your mind on things above' is sometimes misread as an escape from earthly concern — a kind of spiritual navel-gazing that retreats from the world into pious contemplation. But Paul's point is precisely the opposite. It is because your life is hidden with Christ in God — because your identity, your security, your destiny are anchored in the risen Lord rather than in the fluctuating circumstances of earthly life — that you are free to be fully present in and for the world without being enslaved by it.

The person whose identity depends on their career will be devastated by failure at work. The person whose security depends on their relationships will be destroyed by rejection. The person whose hope depends on their health will be undone by illness. But the person whose life is 'hidden with Christ in God' has a security that earthly circumstances cannot reach. This is not detachment — it is the freedom that comes from having your roots in the right soil.

The eschatological promise at the end — 'when Christ is revealed, you will also be revealed with him in glory' — gives the Christian life its forward direction. We are living toward a disclosure, a revealing of what we already secretly are in Christ. The resurrection life we now live hidden will one day be made fully public, fully visible, fully glorious.
sections.prayer: Risen Lord, help us to live from the security of resurrection rather than toward it. Our lives are hidden with you. Our identity is fixed in you. Our future is guaranteed by you. Free us from the anxiety of those who have to earn their standing, and fill us with the settled confidence of those who know where they belong. Let us live on earth as those whose true life is above.

sections.title: Resurrection and Suffering
sections.scriptureText: For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory which will be revealed toward us.
sections.reflection:
Paul's confidence in Romans 8:18 is remarkable given his own biography. He who would later write from prison, who had been beaten and shipwrecked and stoned, who knew suffering from the inside, declares that the present sufferings are 'not worth comparing' with coming glory. This is not the optimism of someone who has been spared hardship. It is the calculation of someone who has done the arithmetic and found that glory outweighs all the suffering he has known.

The Greek word for 'I consider' (logizomai) is a mathematical term — it means to reckon, to calculate, to take account of. Paul is not suppressing his suffering or pretending it does not hurt. He is placing it on a scale and finding that the other side is infinitely heavier. The suffering is real. The glory is realer.

N.T. Wright has argued that the resurrection hope in Paul is not a consolation prize that makes present suffering bearable by promising future escape. It is rather the fuel for present engagement and resistance. Because we know that this creation will be redeemed and that our bodies will be raised, the work we do in and for this world is not futile. Paul says at the end of 1 Corinthians 15: 'Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain.'

Suffering does not disprove the resurrection. It is the context within which the resurrection hope burns most brightly. The martyrs of the early church faced death with a serenity that their persecutors could not understand — because they genuinely believed that death was not the end, that the resurrection of Jesus meant their resurrection too, and that the suffering they endured was temporary while the glory it was preparing them for was eternal.
sections.prayer: God of glory, when we are in pain and cannot see past the present moment, recalibrate our arithmetic. Remind us what Paul knew: that these sufferings, as real as they are, are not the final measure. The glory outweighs them. The resurrection outweighs them. Help us to not just believe this but feel the weight of it — enough to be steadfast, enough to endure, enough to abound in your work even when it costs us.

title: Sola Scriptura: The Sufficiency of Scripture
description: What did the Reformers mean by sola scriptura, and how does Scripture's authority relate to tradition, reason, and experience? This study recovers the Reformation's high view of Scripture as God's sufficient word for the church in every age.
outline: The Spirit and the Word Together

sections.title: What the Reformation Recovered
sections.scriptureText: Every Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for instruction in righteousness, that each person who belongs to God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work.
sections.reflection:
When Luther nailed his Ninety-Five Theses in 1517, the most revolutionary element was not his critique of indulgences per se but his implicit appeal to Scripture as the supreme authority over church practice. By the time of the Leipzig Debate in 1519, this had become explicit: Luther declared that councils and popes could err, but Scripture could not. The Reformation slogan sola scriptura — Scripture alone — was the formal principle of the entire movement.

What exactly was being claimed? Sola scriptura does not mean that Scripture is the only authority Christians consult. The Reformers valued tradition, respected the church fathers, and drew on reason. What they insisted is that Scripture is the supreme and final authority — that tradition must be tested by Scripture, not the reverse. When the two conflict, Scripture wins.

Paul's language in 2 Timothy 3:16 is extraordinary: 'God-breathed' (theopneustos). The Scripture did not merely originate with human authors who were inspired in the way great artists or poets are inspired. It was breathed out by God himself — which means it carries his authority, his accuracy, and his purpose. This is not a claim that Paul invented; it is the claim the Old Testament makes repeatedly about itself: 'Thus says the Lord.'

The purpose Paul specifies is thoroughly practical: Scripture is profitable for 'teaching, reproof, correction, and instruction in righteousness' — so that the person of God may be 'complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work.' Scripture is not a repository of interesting religious ideas. It is the divine tool kit for the formation of holy people. Sufficiency means that everything necessary for life and godliness is provided within Scripture's pages.
sections.prayer: God who breathes out your word, give us a high view of what you have given us. Where we have let Scripture become familiar and therefore dull, make it strange again — alive, active, demanding. Where we have supplemented it with our own wisdom until your voice was crowded out, bring us back to the text. We want to be equipped by your word. Do not let us settle for anything less.

sections.title: Scripture as God's Word
sections.scriptureText: For the word of God is living and active, and sharper than any two-edged sword, and piercing even to the dividing of soul and spirit, of both joints and marrow, and is able to discern the thoughts and intentions of the heart.
sections.reflection:
The writer to the Hebrews makes a claim about Scripture that no merely human document could sustain: it is 'living and active.' Not was living — is living. Present tense. Scripture is not a record of what God once said; it is what God is still saying.
